title: Quiche Lorraine
subtitle: Shortcrust case filled with lardons, cream, and egg custard
excerpt: Quiche Lorraine: a blind-baked shortcrust case filled with fried lardons and a savoury egg and cream custard. No cheese in the traditional version. The filling sets to a soft, just-trembling texture. Served warm or at room temperature.
sourceNotes: Quiche Lorraine originates from the Lorraine region of France and is documented in French domestic cookery from at least the sixteenth century, when it appeared as an open tart with bacon and egg custard. The modern standardised form (shortcrust pastry, lardons, cream, and egg custard) is documented in both French and English-language cooking references throughout the twentieth century. Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960), and numerous British adaptations of French regional cooking establish the canonical form. The recipe as given here is fully public-domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen at 160°C fan for 20 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the day before. Reheat at 160°C fan for 10 minutes or serve at room temperature.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven for the filling, after blind-baking at 190°C

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, 30 to 35 minutes until filling is just set with a slight wobble

glossaryTerms.definition: The classic Lorraine ratio is 3 eggs to 300 ml cream, giving a rich, just-set filling that holds its shape when sliced but is not rubbery. Adding more eggs makes the filling stiffer; more cream makes it softer and more prone to weeping. Some modern versions use a mix of cream and milk to reduce richness — if substituting half the cream with whole milk, add one extra yolk to maintain the set. The custard is done when the edges are firm and the centre has a distinct wobble.

content.text: The only cheese that belongs in Quiche Lorraine is the debate about whether cheese belongs in Quiche Lorraine at all. The traditional Lorraine filling is bacon, cream, and eggs. If you want Gruyère, add it (it is excellent), but the classic is dairy-only.

Roll the {{shortcrust-pastry}} to 3 mm and line the 23 cm tart tin. Prick the base, cover, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 190°C fan. Line the case with baking paper and baking beans. Blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 5 minutes. Reduce the oven to 170°C fan.
Cook the lardons, make the custard

Fry the {{lardons}} in a dry pan over medium heat until the fat is rendered and the edges are lightly coloured. Drain on kitchen paper. Whisk the {{eggs}}, {{egg-yolks}}, and {{double-cream}} together. Season with salt, pepper, and a fine grating of {{nutmeg-whole}}. See the

Scatter the lardons evenly over the pastry base. Pour the custard over to just below the rim. Bake at 170°C fan for 30 to 35 minutes until the edges are set and the centre has a slight wobble. Leave to cool in the tin for 30 minutes before serving.
